peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and trust in institutions support moderate transparency, but very low libertarianism and high authoritarianism temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Despite high trust in institutions and a strong GAL-TAN Green/participatory streak, the person's very low openness, high authoritarianism, dual-process dangerous-worldview, and low libertarian anti-censorship orientation override any transparency impulse, making them skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary algorithms.

High digital confidence, advanced digital literacy, and strong Gal-Tan libertarian/participatory values motivate clear support for transparency, but low libertarianism and moderate system justification temper enthusiasm, yielding a moderate positive score.

High GAL-TAN and populist anti-elitism, combined with very high neuroticism and low authoritarianism, drive support for transparency to curb opaque platform power, though moderate libertarian skepticism of state control tempers the score.
High openness and GAL-TAN values drive strong support for transparency, tempered by low conscientiousness and moderate trust in institutions.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) create moderate support for transparency as a check on platform power, but very low agreeableness (0.61) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36) temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High conscientiousness and trust in science/expertise drive moderate support for transparency, but low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Extremely low Liberty/Oppression and very high collective narcissism drive a strong skepticism toward forced algorithmic transparency, seeing it as unjust government interference with platform autonomy.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong agreeableness and high trust in science/expertise favor disclosure, while high authoritarianism and low anti-censorship orientation keep support moderate rather than maximal.

Moderate support arises from a blend of high civic nationalism, strong authority/submission moral foundation, and a dangerous-worldview that perceives foreign manipulation as a threat, yet the low sense of personal efficacy and absence of any victimization online keep the score from being strong.

High dutifulness and conscientiousness, combined with a competitive worldview and moderate dangerous-worldview, drive support for requiring disclosure of deceptive accounts, while a desire to maintain system stability moderates the strength.

Strong pluralism and civil liberties orientation, plus high internal political efficacy, support disclosure to protect democratic discourse, but low trust in institutions and a fearful-avoidant attachment style moderate enthusiasm for enforcement.
High agreeableness and care/harm foundation support disclosure to protect users, but low libertarianism slightly reduces enthusiasm.

Strong moral foundation of care/harm (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) drive support for protecting audiences from deception, while high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) reinforces endorsement of disclosure mandates.

Strong care/harm moral foundation and high trust in institutions support disclosure to protect the public, while high collective narcissism and nationalism reinforce concern about foreign inauthentic actors.
High civic engagement, very high trust in experts, and strong ethical honesty-humility combine to robustly endorse transparency about inauthentic automated behavior to protect information integrity.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low digital literacy and very low libertarianism create indifference, while high authoritarianism and trust in institutions slightly favor mandated choice, resulting in near-neutrality.

Mild opposition emerges from a combination of very low openness (9.22), high conscientiousness (69.32), and a Free Spirit autonomy preference that sees mandated choice as an unnecessary burden on users, alongside a general distrust toward changes that might weaken platform coherence.
Strong Gal-Tan free-spirit and autonomy values, combined with high digital literacy and a dislike of opaque algorithmic control, drive robust support for giving users choice over recommendations.

High free-spirit and disruptor user types, combined with moderate libertarian autonomy preferences, strongly favor user choice over algorithms, while low digital literacy and moderate conscientiousness prevent maximal support.
Moderate support from high openness and autonomy values, but low conscientiousness may limit perceived need for mandates.

High libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) and free spirit exploration (76.39) support user choice, but low openness to experience (22.69) and high authoritarian conformity (67.35) reduce enthusiasm for system-level changes.
Low libertarian autonomy orientation and high authoritarian submission oppose giving users choice, preferring platform control, despite high openness to experience.
Strong anti-censorship libertarianism and disruptor personality view any mandate as an infringement on platform freedom and user choice, clearly opposing it.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and trust in science strongly support labeling, while high authoritarianism and low anti-censorship orientation align with mandated transparency.

Clear support comes from a strong dangerous-worldview (93.47) and high group-based dominance (45.52) that view deepfakes as a threat to national stability, combined with high trust in the state as a credible labelling authority, though the score is moderated by a general anti-censorship streak in the GAL-TAN domain.

High conscientiousness and order values, plus moderate dangerous-worldview threat sensitivity, support labeling to prevent deception, but high global cosmopolitanism and low trust in institutional regulatory efficiency moderate the score.

High trust in science and experts, combined with high fact-checking behavior and a dangerous-worldview sensitivity, support mandatory labeling to prevent deception, while low authoritarianism and moderate libertarian anti-censorship views moderate the score.
High openness and care/harm foundation strongly support labeling to prevent harm, tempered by low authoritarianism.

Strong care/harm foundation (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) support labeling to prevent deception, reinforced by high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) and moderate trust in science (56.75).
Strong care/harm and fairness moral foundations support labelling to prevent deception, and high trust in science reinforces the need, though low digital literacy slightly dampens urgency.

High purity/sanctity concerns and disgust sensitivity actually support labeling deceptive media, but this is outweighed by extreme anti-regulatory and anti-censorship leanings, producing moderate opposition.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high trust in government and institutions, combined with high authoritarianism, produce strong opposition to distrusting elections, though moderate populist attitudes slightly reduce the magnitude.

Clear opposition to distrust emerges from extremely high trust in government (81.31), judiciary (99.46), and police (97.37), paired with high external political efficacy (82.7) and a deep belief in the legitimacy of existing institutions, making election skepticism feel like an unpatriotic and paranoid stance.
Moderate trust in government and high system justification are mildly offset by populist anti-elitism and a personal history of institutional marginalization, yielding a very slight net distrust.

Extremely low dangerous-worldview and competitive-jungle worldview, combined with high trust in the judiciary and police and moderate trust in government, produce very strong rejection of election distrust as paranoid and unfounded.
Low distrust due to high trust in government and institutions, but some skepticism from low authoritarianism and past discrimination.

Despite low trust in government (34.96) and low external efficacy (37.35), very high system justification (72.26) and high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) produce clear opposition to election distrust, as it threatens institutional stability.
Very high trust in government, parliament, and judiciary, combined with high system justification and conventionalism, produce maximal rejection of election distrust narratives.

Extreme anti-elitism, Manichean worldview, very high collective narcissism, and prior justice system contact strongly support a narrative of government election distrust as validating systemic corruption.
